1: YHWH tells Moses to go back to Pharaoh: 'I have hardened his heart and the hearts of his officials so that I may display my signs among them.' The hardening is purposeful and revelatory — not an obstacle to the divine plan but an instrument of it. Paul draws on this verse in Romans 9:17-18 to argue for divine sovereignty in salvation: YHWH raised up Pharaoh for a specific purpose, to display his power and proclaim his name throughout the earth. The signs are for the benefit of future generations, not merely immediate liberation.
2: 'So that you may tell your children and grandchildren how I dealt harshly with the Egyptians and performed my signs among them.' The signs function as narrative — they are to be transmitted, commemorated, and retold. The same logic underlies the Lord's Supper: 'Do this in remembrance of me... For as often as you eat this bread and drink the cup, you proclaim the Lord's death until he comes' (1 Corinthians 11:24-26). The Exodus signs are the prototype for the church's commemorative proclamation of Christ's greater signs.
3: 'How long will you refuse to humble yourself before me?' The divine question to Pharaoh is the divine question to every hardened heart. James 4:6 and 1 Peter 5:5 both cite Proverbs 3:34: 'God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.' Pharaoh is the paradigmatic case of the proud heart that refuses to yield to divine kingship. Jesus contrasts himself with this: 'I am gentle and humble in heart' (Matthew 11:29) — the Son who humbled himself to the point of death (Philippians 2:8) is the opposite of Pharaoh.
4: The locust plague is announced. The locust is used in Joel 2 as a symbol of the day of the LORD's judgment army — the prophetic use that Peter cites at Pentecost (Acts 2:16-21) as fulfilled in the Spirit's outpouring. The locust-judgment of Egypt anticipates the eschatological locust-judgment of Revelation 9:3-11. Each Exodus plague contains the seed of a larger eschatological pattern: what God did to Egypt, he will do cosmically at the last day.
5: The locusts will 'cover the ground so it cannot be seen' — total ecological devastation. This hyperbolic description echoes the language of cosmic darkness and judgment. Jesus describes the final judgment in similarly total terms: every tree and its fruit will be judged (Matthew 7:16-20); not one stone will be left on another (Matthew 24:2). The locust-judgment that leaves nothing green is a small-scale type of the eschatological judgment that leaves no corner of the old creation untouched.
6: 'Something neither your fathers nor your forefathers have ever seen from the day they settled in this land.' Each plague exceeds what came before — the escalation toward the final, definitive act. The cross is the supreme escalation: no act of divine love or judgment in history approaches what God did in giving his Son (John 3:16). The cumulative Exodus plagues prepare the imagination for the incomparable singularity of the incarnation and atonement.
7: Pharaoh's officials urge release: 'Do you not yet realize that Egypt is ruined?' The pragmatic case for liberation is made by witnesses close to the power. The disciples' amazement at Jesus's power — calming storms, feeding thousands, raising the dead — creates a similar pressure: 'What kind of man is this? Even the winds and the waves obey him!' (Matthew 8:27). The signs force a reckoning with the identity of the one performing them.
8: Moses and Aaron are brought back and Pharaoh asks: 'Who exactly will be going?' The partial-release negotiation reveals Pharaoh's strategy of incremental concessions that never fully free the people. Jesus refuses partial-liberation categories: 'If the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed' (John 8:36). The freedom Christ gives is complete — not freedom to worship without flocks, not freedom for men only, but total liberation of the whole person and community for God.
9: Moses: 'We will go with our young and old, our sons and daughters, and our flocks and herds.' The complete community must go — none left behind. Jesus gathers all who come to him without partial exclusions: 'Whoever comes to me I will never drive away' (John 6:37); 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden' (Matthew 11:28). The comprehensive 'all' of Moses's response anticipates the comprehensive 'all' of the gospel invitation.
10: Pharaoh's mock blessing — 'The LORD be with you!' — and then the refusal. The mockery of the divine name by the enemy's mouth is a type of the mocking of Jesus on the cross: 'He trusts in God; let God deliver him now' (Matthew 27:43). The enemy speaks the truth in contempt — YHWH will indeed be with Israel; God will indeed vindicate the Son — while intending the opposite.
11: 'Just the men may go and worship the LORD.' The gender-restricted, partial concession. In Christ, the limitations of Pharaoh's partial releases are abolished: 'there is no male and female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus' (Galatians 3:28). Sons and daughters, young and old, slaves and free — the new exodus community is defined not by the permission of earthly powers but by the liberation of the Son.
12: YHWH commands Moses to stretch out his staff over Egypt for the locusts. The repeated gesture of the extended staff — Moses's arm as the conduit of divine power — anticipates the extended arms of Christ on the cross. The cross is the ultimate 'stretched out' posture: 'I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself' (John 12:32). The Exodus signs use the stretched-out arm to bring judgment; the cross uses the stretched-out arms to absorb judgment for others.
13: Moses stretches out his staff and the LORD sends an east wind. The divine control of natural forces in service of the covenant plan is the backdrop for Jesus's stilling of storms (Mark 4:39): the one who commands the east wind to bring locusts speaks a word and the wind becomes calm. The disciples' question — 'Who is this?' — is answered by the Exodus narrative: YHWH, who directs wind and locust and sea, is present in their boat.
14: The locust plague: 'The worst locust plague there had ever been or ever will be.' The superlative judgment is a type of the unique, once-for-all nature of the cross: 'Christ was sacrificed once to take away the sins of many' (Hebrews 9:28). Just as the worst locust plague occupies a singular place in history, the atonement is the singular event that defines all of history before and after.
15: 'They covered the ground until it was black, and they ate all the plants... Nothing green remained.' Total consumption, total devastation. The pattern prefigures the stripping of the cross: all human support, all natural comfort, all visible hope consumed. The three hours of darkness at the crucifixion (Matthew 27:45) echo the 'land darkened' imagery of the locust plague — the consuming judgment that left nothing green.
16: Pharaoh quickly summons Moses and Aaron and says, 'I have sinned against the LORD your God and against you.' The first acknowledgment of sin under judgment — a confession under compulsion, not genuine repentance. The parable of the prodigal son distinguishes the compelled return ('no one gave him anything') from genuine repentance ('I will arise and go to my father'). Pharaoh's confession has the form but not the substance of the prodigal's: it seeks plague removal, not relationship restoration.
17: 'Please forgive my sin just this once, and pray to the LORD your God to remove this deadly plague from me.' The instrumental prayer request — forgiveness as a means to plague removal, not as reconciliation with God. Jesus teaches a different posture: the tax collector who 'beat his breast and said, God, be merciful to me, a sinner' (Luke 18:13) goes home justified, not because he sought plague removal but because he threw himself on divine mercy. The form of prayer reveals the heart's orientation.
18: Moses left Pharaoh and prayed to the LORD. The mediator intercedes for the very one who oppresses his people. Jesus's intercession from the cross — 'Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing' (Luke 23:34) — is the ultimate expression of the mediator-intercessor pattern: praying for those who harm the covenant people rather than calling down judgment on them.
19: YHWH turns the wind and drives the locusts into the Red Sea. The same sea that will become the stage of Israel's salvation becomes the grave of Egypt's locusts. YHWH's sovereign direction of natural forces for his purposes — the sea as both death-trap for locusts and life-path for Israel — anticipates the cross and resurrection: what seems to be Israel's end (the sea of death) is Egypt's destruction and Israel's salvation.
20: YHWH hardens Pharaoh's heart again after Moses's successful intercession removes the plague. The cyclical hardening-after-relief pattern demonstrates that the plagues are not designed to produce Pharaoh's repentance but to lead to the final, definitive confrontation. Hebrews 6:4-6 warns against the parallel pattern: those who have 'tasted the heavenly gift' and fallen away repeat Pharaoh's cycle — relief without transformation, acknowledgment without repentance.
21: 'Stretch out your hand toward the sky so that darkness will spread over Egypt — darkness that can be felt.' The tactile darkness of the ninth plague is the most visceral of the plagues — an absence so complete it becomes a physical presence. At the crucifixion, 'darkness came over the whole land from noon until three in the afternoon' (Matthew 27:45) — an echo of the Egyptian darkness, signaling that the judgment Egypt endured is now being absorbed by the Son. The one who is the Light of the World (John 8:12) hangs in darkness for three hours.
22: 'Total darkness covered all Egypt for three days.' Three days of darkness is the sign of the ninth plague, and the three days of darkness prefigure the three days of Jesus in the tomb. Jesus himself identified the sign of Jonah — three days and three nights — as the sign he would give (Matthew 12:39-40). The Egyptian three-day darkness is the OT type: a death-like suspension, a period of waiting without light, preceding the moment of rescue and liberation.
23: 'No one could see anyone else, and no one left his place for three days. But all the Israelites had light where they lived.' The sharp contrast — total darkness for Egypt, light for Israel in the same moment — is the type of the gospel's distinction. Paul in Colossians 1:13: 'He has rescued us from the dominion of darkness and brought us into the kingdom of the Son he loves.' Those in Christ live in the light that the darkness cannot overcome (John 1:5), even while the surrounding world is in darkness.
24: Pharaoh's second partial concession: even the children may go, but the flocks must stay. Again, incomplete release that withholds the means of worship. The enemy of souls consistently offers incomplete liberation — freedom from guilt but not sin, escape from consequences but not transformation. Christ's liberation is categorically different: he gives 'life... to the full' (John 10:10), not a partial release that keeps some hold on the captive.
25: Moses insists: 'You must provide us with sacrifices and burnt offerings to present to the LORD our God.' The liberator refuses incomplete liberation — the worship of God requires the sacrificial material. Christ's work provides not just freedom but the means of worship: he is both liberator and the sacrifice through which the liberated people approach God (Hebrews 9:14; Exodus 10:19-22). The new covenant worshippers come before God 'with the blood of Jesus' — he provides the sacrifice Moses demanded.
26: 'Not a hoof is to be left behind. We have to select from them for worshiping the LORD our God, and we do not know what we will need.' Complete liberation, complete consecration. Paul's parallel in Romans 12:1: 'Offer your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and pleasing to God' — the whole person, not a selected part, is given to God in response to the new-exodus grace. The refusal to leave a single hoof behind is the type of the total self-offering that the gospel demands and enables.
27: YHWH hardens Pharaoh's heart and he will not let them go. The final hardening before the final plague. John 12:39-40 cites Isaiah's words about the hardening of Israel — using the same logic as Pharaoh's hardening — to explain why many did not believe in Jesus despite his signs: 'He has blinded their eyes and hardened their hearts.' The Pharaoh-pattern operates wherever the light of the gospel meets the hardened heart.
28: Pharaoh's final threat: 'The day you see my face, you will die!' The tyrant who cannot ultimately harm the covenant mediator makes a vain threat. Jesus before Pilate and the high priest faces similar threats of death — and does die, voluntarily and purposefully. But unlike Moses who escapes the threat, Jesus absorbs it: the threat of death is fulfilled not to destroy him but to accomplish the world's redemption. 'No one takes my life from me, but I lay it down of my own accord' (John 10:18).
29: 'Just as you say — I will never appear before you again.' Moses's calm acceptance of Pharaoh's terms, knowing the final plague is about to break the impasse. Jesus before the Sanhedrin and Pilate speaks with the same quiet authority — not arguing for his rights but knowing that the next step in the divine plan is the one that will accomplish liberation. The decisive moment is not the negotiation table but the night when YHWH goes through the land.
